section.title: Final Greetings
section.sectionRationale: Paul closes the letter as he opened it, with peace and grace. He prays that the Lord of peace Himself would give the troubled church peace at all times and in every way, adds his own handwritten signature as a safeguard against the forged letters that had unsettled them, and ends with the grace of the Lord Jesus for all. The benediction gathers up the whole letter's comfort into a few weighty lines.
section.thesis: The Lord of peace Himself is the church's settled hope, giving peace in every circumstance; Paul's own hand authenticates the true apostolic word against every counterfeit, and the letter ends where the gospel always rests, in the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ.
section.frameNote: One brief movement: the prayer for peace from the Lord of peace, the authenticating signature in Paul's own hand, and the closing grace of the Lord Jesus for all.

units.label: Peace, signature, and grace
units.note: the closing benediction of the letter

verses.text: Now may the Lord of {{eirene|peace}} Himself give you peace at all times and in every way. The Lord be with all of you.
This greeting is in my own hand, Paul. This is my {{semeion|mark}} in every letter; it is the way I write.
The {{charis|grace}} of our Lord Jesus Christ be with all of you.

eirene.gloss: Peace, wholeness, well-being; the Greek word carries the full weight of the Hebrew shalom, a settled flourishing under God. Paul calls the Lord Himself the Lord of peace and prays He would give peace at all times and in every way. For a church rattled by persecution and false alarms, this is peace as a gift of God's own presence, not merely the absence of trouble.

semeion.gloss: A sign, mark, or token of authenticity. Paul points to his own handwriting as the distinguishing mark of a genuine letter in every one he sends. Given the forged or misattributed letter that had alarmed the church, this signature is a safeguard, certifying the true apostolic word against counterfeits.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's undeserved favor and saving kindness. Paul opened the letter with grace and now closes with it, praying the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ on all of them. Grace frames the whole letter, so that even its warnings and commands rest within the free, sustaining favor of Christ.

basic.context: Paul ends his letter the way he began, with peace and grace. To a church shaken by persecution and by a false report that the Day of the Lord had come, he prays that the Lord of peace Himself would give them peace in every situation. He then signs the letter in his own handwriting, a deliberate safeguard because a forged letter had helped cause the confusion he corrected in chapter two. His final word is a blessing of the grace of the Lord Jesus on everyone in the church.
basic.hermeneutics: The closing benediction is more than a formality; each line answers the letter's concerns. The prayer for peace speaks to their anxiety, the signature answers the problem of forged letters, and the grace blessing frames the whole. Reading it well means hearing these lines as the letter's pastoral heartbeat, not skipping them as a routine sign-off.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul calls the Lord the Lord of eirene, peace, the Greek word carrying the full weight of Hebrew shalom, wholeness under God, and prays for it at all times and in every way. He points to his own handwriting as the semeion, the authenticating mark, in every letter, a guard against forgery. He closes with charis, grace, the same word that opened the letter, so grace frames the whole.
basic.typology: The title Lord of peace echoes the Old Testament promise of the Prince of Peace and the LORD who grants shalom to His people. Jesus, the risen Lord, is the giver of that promised peace, present with His church at all times.
basic.christ: Christ is the Lord of peace who gives peace and is present with His people, and the letter ends in the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ. To ask that the Lord be with all of you and that His grace rest on all is to treat the risen Jesus as the ever-present source of peace and favor, a fitting close that centers the whole letter on Him.
basic.god: God is the Lord of peace, personally present with His people and generous with peace in every circumstance. He is a God of grace from the letter's first line to its last, whose favor surrounds even His corrections. The peace and grace He gives are gifts of His own presence, not merely improved circumstances.
basic.crossReferences: First Thessalonians 5 closes with the same prayer that the God of peace would sanctify them wholly. Romans 15 calls Him the God of peace be with you all. And John 14 records Jesus giving His own peace to His troubled disciples, the peace the Lord of peace now grants this church.
basic.oneStory: The letter, and the gospel, rests at last in peace and grace. The Lord of peace who was promised of old and came in Jesus gives His people a settled peace amid every trouble, authenticates His true word against every counterfeit, and surrounds them with grace, a foretaste of the unbroken peace of the new creation where God dwells with His people forever.

context.literary: The benediction mirrors the letter's opening greeting of grace and peace, forming a frame around the whole. Its three lines move from a prayer for peace, to the authenticating signature, to the closing grace, compact but deliberate. The emphatic Himself in the Lord of peace Himself stresses that peace comes from the personal presence of the Lord.
context.historical: Paul typically dictated his letters to a secretary and then added a few words in his own hand. Here that practice becomes a security measure: because a letter falsely attributed to him had unsettled the Thessalonians, verse 2 of chapter two, his handwriting serves as a verifiable mark of authenticity for this and every genuine letter.
context.canonical: The closing takes its place among Paul's characteristic grace-benedictions and echoes the peace-benedictions found across his letters. Its confession of the risen Lord as the giver of peace and grace fits the New Testament's steady witness to the present lordship of the exalted Christ over His church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To leave a troubled church with the settled peace of the Lord's own presence, to certify this letter as genuinely his against the forgeries that had caused confusion, and to seal the whole letter in the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The lines are chiefly prayer and attestation rather than command. The prayers for peace and grace express what Paul asks God to give, and the signature is a statement of fact about his practice. Their abiding force is pastoral: they teach the church where true peace and authority are found, in the present Lord and His authentic word.

debates.question: Why does Paul draw attention to his own handwriting in verse 17?

views.label: To authenticate against forgery
views.case: Chapter two mentions a letter seeming to be from Paul that had alarmed the church; his handwritten mark in every letter guards them from being deceived again by a counterfeit.

views.label: To underline apostolic authority
views.case: Beyond mere verification, the personal signature stamps the letter's commands with Paul's own apostolic weight, reinforcing that its teaching is to be received and obeyed.

debates.bearing: Either way the signature ties the church's peace to the trustworthy apostolic word, protecting them from deception and grounding their confidence in genuine Scripture.

debates.question: What is the peace the Lord of peace gives at all times and in every way?

views.label: Inner, God-given wholeness amid trouble
views.case: The peace is a gift of the Lord's own presence, shalom that steadies the heart in persecution and confusion, not dependent on calm circumstances.

views.label: Also peace within the community
views.case: Given the letter's concern with disorderly members, the prayer likely includes restored harmony in the church as well as peace in each believer's heart; the two go together.

debates.bearing: The prayer promises a peace rooted in God Himself, sufficient in every circumstance and reaching both the anxious heart and the strained community.

study.originalLanguages: Paul names the Lord as the Lord of eirene, and the Greek word gathers up the whole Hebrew idea of shalom, a settled wholeness and flourishing under God, not the mere absence of conflict; this peace He gives at all times and in every way. The emphatic Himself underlines that peace flows from the Lord's own presence. Paul then points to his own handwriting as the semeion, the mark or token of authenticity, in every letter, transforming an ordinary scribal custom into a safeguard against the forged letter that had unsettled the church. He ends with charis, grace, the very word that opened the letter, so that the whole is framed by the free favor of the Lord Jesus Christ.

typology.fulfillment: Takes up the promise of the Prince of Peace and the LORD who grants shalom, now fulfilled in the risen Lord Jesus who gives His people peace at all times.
Reflects the pattern of God's word given within His gracious favor, brought to fullness as the apostolic letter opens and closes in the grace of Christ.

study.christ: The close centers on the risen Christ. He is the Lord of peace who Himself gives peace, He is the Lord whose presence Paul invokes over all of them, and He is the source of the grace that ends the letter. To pray that the Lord be with all of you and that His grace rest on all is to confess Jesus as personally present and actively gracious to His church, the fitting summit of a letter that has fixed the church's hope on His coming and His care.
study.god: God is the Lord of peace, near to His people and generous with peace in every time and circumstance. From its opening grace and peace to its closing grace, the letter shows a God whose favor surrounds His people even in His warnings and commands. The peace He gives is the gift of His own presence, and the grace He grants is the atmosphere in which the whole Christian life is lived.

crossReferences.note: May the God of peace Himself sanctify you completely, the same closing prayer to the God of peace for this church.
The God of peace be with all of you, a parallel peace-benediction from Paul.
Jesus gives His own peace to troubled disciples, the very peace the Lord of peace grants here.
I, Paul, write this greeting in my own hand, the same authenticating signature practice.

study.oneStory: The letter ends where the whole gospel finally rests, in peace and grace. The Lord of peace promised through the prophets and come in Jesus gives His people a settled peace amid persecution and confusion, guards them with His true word against every counterfeit, and surrounds them with His grace. It is a small foretaste of the end of the story, the unbroken peace of the new creation where the Lord Himself is with His people and grace has the last word.

quiz.q: What does Paul pray for the church in verse 16?

options.text: That the Lord of peace Himself would give them peace at all times and in every way.
options.note: Verse 16 prays for peace from the Lord of peace in every circumstance.

options.text: That they would finally get revenge on their persecutors.
options.note: The prayer is for peace, not revenge.

options.text: That they would become wealthy and untroubled.
options.note: The peace prayed for is God's gift in every circumstance, not material ease.

options.text: That they would be left to find peace on their own.
options.note: The peace comes from the Lord of peace Himself, not from their own efforts.

quiz.q: Why does Paul add a greeting in his own hand in verse 17?

options.text: As a mark of authenticity in every letter, guarding against forged letters.
options.note: Verse 17 gives his handwriting as the distinguishing mark against counterfeits.

options.text: Because he did not trust anyone else to deliver the letter.
options.note: The point is authentication of the letter itself, not distrust of a courier.

options.text: To show off his handwriting skill.
options.note: The signature is a safeguard of authenticity, not a display.

options.text: Because he had forgotten to sign his other letters.
options.note: He says this mark appears in every letter; it is a deliberate practice, not an oversight.

quiz.q: How does the letter end, in verse 18?

options.text: With the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with all of you.
options.note: Verse 18 closes with the grace of Christ for all, framing the letter.

options.text: With a stern final warning of judgment.
options.note: The letter ends on grace, not on a warning.

options.text: With a list of names to be excluded.
options.note: It ends with grace for all of you, not with exclusion.
The close is a grace-benediction, not a financial appeal.

quiz.q: What does the title Lord of peace, with the emphatic Himself, stress in verse 16?

options.text: That true peace comes from the personal presence of the Lord.
options.note: The emphatic Himself points to peace flowing from the Lord's own presence.

options.text: That peace is merely the absence of external conflict.
options.note: The biblical peace is fuller wholeness rooted in the Lord, not just calm circumstances.

options.text: That the church must manufacture its own peace.
options.note: Peace is the Lord's gift, given by His presence, not self-manufactured.

options.text: That peace will only come after death.
options.note: He gives peace at all times and in every way, now as well as forever.

questions: Paul prays for peace at all times and in every way from the Lord of peace Himself. In what circumstance right now do you most need to receive peace as a gift of the Lord's presence rather than a change in your situation?

The whole letter is framed by grace, from its opening line to its last. How does knowing that even God's warnings and commands come wrapped in His grace change the way you read hard passages of Scripture?

Paul guarded the church against counterfeit messages by pointing to the authentic apostolic word. How do you test the many voices and messages competing for your attention against the trustworthy Scriptures?

The letter ends with a blessing of grace on all of you, not a select few. Who in your church family most needs to hear that the grace of the Lord Jesus is genuinely for them, and how could you tell them?

translationNotes.note: The Lord of peace renders a title that draws on the Hebrew idea of shalom, wholeness and flourishing under God; the emphatic Himself stresses that peace comes from the Lord's own presence.

This greeting is in my own hand, Paul reflects Paul's practice of adding a handwritten note to a dictated letter; here it serves to authenticate the letter against forgery. An em dash in the source was rendered as a comma per house style.
Some later manuscripts (BYZ and TR) add Amen at the end; the BSB follows the earlier reading without it.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Thessalonians 3:16-18. The em dash in verse 17 was rendered as a comma per house style. All three verses covered contiguously.
